segments.text:
Lab 4 Tree Diversity & Ecological Succession
Secondary succession – Start with area that has been significantly disturbed

Lab 4 Tree Diversity & Ecological Succession
GOALS:
Introduce methods for identification of local tree species
Study how disturbance can affect the species composition and age structure of a local plant community
Learn approaches to estimating biodiversity in a community
Introduction to field sampling techniques

Lab 3 Tree Diversity & Ecological Succession
ACTIVITIES:
Meet at Rum Village Nature Center
Introduce lab methods
Collect tree diversity data
Collate data and make sure everyone has a full set of data before leaving Rum Village

Rum Village
IUSB
Travel to Rum Village
Address: 2626 S Gertrude Street South Bend, Indiana 46614
Address: https://what3words.com/takes.deeply.enjoy [https://what3words.com/takes.deeply.enjoy]

Nature Center
(park here)

Disturbed area
Undisturbed area

Rum Village:
Collect data on tree age and tree diversity in two habitats
Recently cut forest
Disturbed
Older forested area
Undisturbed
More mature forest, but with several recent smaller disturbances due to wind storms
Forest in earlier stage of succession – recovering from being a mowed area ~35 yrs ago

We will be examining…
Species composition
Species richness
Tree relative abundance
Age structure
Techniques required…
Sampling
Tree recognition
Data processing

Setting up study plots
Flag toss to determine plot location
Plots will be 100 m 2 in area
2 10-m tapes per plot intersecting to form a cross
Start from the middle; determine boundaries with flags
Plots will have 4 5-m x 5-m quadrants – one student works in each quadrant
5 m
5 m
5 m
5 m

Sampling trees
Work in your assigned quadrant
Identify all trees that are >1.5 m tall
Measure trunk diameter at 1.5 m height & place trees in one of three age categories:
Seedling:  <2.5 cm diameter
Sapling:  2.5-10 cm diameter
Adult:  >10 cm diameter

STEP 1: Simple or compound leaves?
Using leaf traits to identify trees
Simple leaves have a petiole and a single blade.
Compound leaves have a petiole and a blade divided into several leaflets.
Most common compound-leaved trees at Rum Village:  Ash & Hickory.

STEP 1: Simple or compound leaves?
Using leaf traits to identify trees
Ironwood:  Simple leaves
Hickory:  Compound leaves

opposite
alternate
STEP 2:  Leaves and twigs alternate or opposite ?
Using leaf traits to identify trees
Most common opposite-leaved trees at Rum Village:  Ash & Maple.

Using leaf traits to identify trees
STEP 3:  Use photos & tree key to ID tree based on additional features of leaves, twigs, and bark

Trees with compound leaves
Ash
(compound, opposite)
Hickory
(compound, alternate)
Walnut

Ash vs. Hickory – difference in leaf size and arrangement
Ash
leaves opposite
Hickory
leaves alternate

maples (sugar maple – Oh Canada!)
tulip tree (our state tree)
sassafras (0, 2, 3 lobes)
oaks (white vs red)
Trees with simple, lobed leaves

Red Oak vs White Oak
Red Oak
White oak
Red oak = leaf lobes are pointed, with bristle tip
White oak = leaf lobes rounded
[Speaker Notes] White oak: Leaves have 7 to 9 lobes and smooth margins. In the fall look for the leaves to turn a wine-red color then fade to various shades of brown. Notice the light-gray bark which forms broad scaly plates. The acorn is egg shaped and 3/4" long.

Trees with simple leaves and toothed margins
Smooth, entire leaf margin
Toothed leaf margin

Simple leaf with toothed margin (beech, ironwood, black cherry)
Beech
Lvs smooth, long “cigar-shaped” buds
Ironwood
Double serrations, small buds
Black cherry
Smooth leaf w/ fine teeth; bitter almond odor if scratch twig bark

Simple leaf with toothed margin (beech, black cherry)
Beech
Smooth, grey, “Susie loves Johnny” bark
Black cherry
Dark bark with horizontal striations or curved flakes

Simple leaf with toothed margin (basswood, elm, ironwood)
Basswood
Large, round lvs; assymmetric base
Elm
Coarse toothed, oval leaf;  asymmetric base
Ironwood
Double serrations, base not asymmetric

Poison Ivy
Trifoliate leaf (3 leaflets)
Grow on forest ground or climb on trees
Vine can be woody
